description: Golden benchmark set for evaluating whether an agent can apply the intel-performance-skills workflows to practical Linux performance diagnosis and optimization scenarios.
version_or_doc_snapshot: intel/intel-performance-skills main branch, inspected 2026-06-12. Skills covered: linux-perf, performance-patterns, phoronix-test-suite.

questions.text: Scenario: A CPU-bound command `./encode --threads=1` is slow. `perf stat` reports IPC 0.55, cache miss rate 0.3%, branch miss rate 0.7%, and kernel time 2%. What performance regime should the agent infer, which workflow should it run next, and which premature conclusions should it avoid?
expected_topics: Low IPC with low cache, branch, and kernel signals points to a compute or dependency-chain stall rather than memory, branch, or syscall domination.
Run hotspot profiling with `perf record`/`perf report` and then inspect annotated hot functions before proposing a code change.
Do not recommend cache blocking, branch hints, or I/O changes without evidence; consider serial accumulators or narrow SIMD only after source/profile evidence.

metadata.expected_workflow: Flow A to Flow B escalation
metadata.why_it_matters: Tests whether the agent interprets counters as diagnostic signals instead of matching keywords to generic optimization advice.

questions.text: Task: The user asks for source-line hotspots from a stripped production binary. Describe the safe profiling plan, including debug-symbol handling and what must happen to any existing `perf.data` after a rebuild.
expected_topics: Check for DWARF debug information before expensive collection using the skill's debug-symbol building block.
If source is available, offer to rebuild with `-g` while preserving the existing optimization and build flags.
If the binary is rebuilt, discard/re-record existing `perf.data` because addresses no longer match the binary.

metadata.why_it_matters: Prevents plausible but unusable reports that contain addresses with no actionable source mapping.

questions.text: Scenario: Source contains `for (size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) sum += a[i] * b[i];`. No profile has been collected yet. Should the agent wait for `perf annotate`, or invoke a pattern workflow now? Explain the expected diagnosis and verification.
expected_topics: A single scalar accumulator in a hot loop is a strong source-level trigger for the serial accumulator pattern.
Invoke the performance-patterns skill early and read the parallel-accumulator guidance before rewriting.
Verify with before/after benchmark and, when available, perf counters showing improved IPC or reduced accumulator bottleneck.

metadata.why_it_matters: Checks whether the skill helps identify practical opportunities before over-spending on profiling.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf stat` reports IPC 0.7, cache miss rate 18%, branch miss rate 1%, and low kernel time. What should the agent conclude, and what classes of fixes are reasonable to investigate first?
expected_topics: Low IPC plus high cache miss rate indicates a memory-bound workload.
Investigate data layout, cache locality, blocking, prefetching, and working-set behavior before instruction-level tuning.
Avoid treating the problem as branch-heavy, syscall-bound, or purely SIMD-width limited without more evidence.

metadata.why_it_matters: Separates evidence-driven diagnosis from generic 'make it vectorized' advice.

questions.text: Task: A user says: "Profile and optimize `pts/compress-zstd`." What should the agent do before running any manual `perf` or build command, and why?
expected_topics: Immediately invoke the phoronix-test-suite skill when the target is a `pts/<name>` benchmark.
Let that skill resolve/install/run the test and handle source extraction, rebuild flags, binary deployment, and result recording.
Avoid manually rebuilding or guessing install paths because PTS benchmarks have skill-owned lifecycle details.

metadata.why_it_matters: Tests cross-skill routing and prevents invalid benchmark modifications.

questions.text: Scenario: A service scales from 1 to 4 cores but plateaus after 8 cores. At 32 cores, `perf report` shows `pthread_mutex_lock` and futex paths. What workflow should the agent run, and what evidence should it collect before proposing a lock change?
expected_topics: Use the core-count scaling analysis workflow with threading inspection, scaling sweep, and dual 1-core versus N-core profiles.
Identify jumping functions and connect them to lock, futex, or c2c evidence before classifying the bottleneck.
Do not jump directly to an rwlock or lock-free rewrite; first determine whether the critical section is read-heavy, true-sharing, false-sharing, or CAS contention.

metadata.why_it_matters: Scaling problems need comparative evidence, not a single high-core profile.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf c2c` shows a cache line with 12% total HITM. Thread A writes offset 0 and thread B writes offset 8 in the same struct. How should the agent classify the problem, what fix shape should it propose, and how should it verify it?
expected_topics: Different threads writing different offsets on the same hot cache line indicates false sharing.
Use the structured false-sharing fix pattern: separate or align/pad independently written fields so they do not share a cache line.
Verify with before/after throughput and reduced HITM/c2c signal, not just by compiling the changed struct.

metadata.why_it_matters: Checks true cache-line reasoning rather than treating all HITM as generic lock contention.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf annotate` shows a hot loop using `xmm` registers on a machine that supports AVX2 but not AVX-512. What should the agent inspect, which pattern applies, and what should it avoid recommending?
expected_topics: Check CPU capabilities to establish the supported vector-width tier.
Classify the loop as narrow SIMD and apply SIMD upconversion guidance toward AVX2/YMM where semantics allow.
Avoid recommending AVX-512 or unguarded ISA-specific code on a CPU that does not support it.

metadata.expected_workflow: narrow SIMD pattern with CPU capability check
metadata.why_it_matters: Tests whether optimization advice is hardware-aware and dispatch-safe.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf stat -e other_assists.avx_to_sse,other_assists.sse_to_avx` reports non-zero AVX-to-SSE assists, and annotate shows an extreme cost on the first SSE instruction after an AVX function. What is the likely pattern and the minimal verification loop?
expected_topics: Classify this as a missing `vzeroupper` AVX-to-SSE transition penalty.
Add `vzeroupper` or `_mm256_zeroupper()` at appropriate AVX exits before returning to SSE code, following the pattern detail file.
Verify that AVX-to-SSE assists drop to zero or near zero and that runtime/cycles improve.

metadata.why_it_matters: Catches a specialized but costly x86 transition issue that generic profilers often miss.

questions.text: Scenario: A C function `void add(float *a, float *b, float *out, int n)` is called only with non-overlapping buffers. Annotate shows a scalar aliasing preamble and two loop versions. What should the agent propose, and what language/verification caveats matter?
expected_topics: Recognize missing `restrict` as the likely source of alias checks or scalar fallback.
Only propose `restrict` when the non-overlap contract is true and document that this is C-specific; C++ uses non-standard `__restrict__` extensions.
Verify by inspecting that the alias preamble/fallback is removed or reduced and by measuring performance.

metadata.why_it_matters: Checks whether the agent can improve vectorization without introducing undefined behavior.

questions.text: Scenario: A spinlock uses a loop that repeatedly performs `lock cmpxchg` until it succeeds. Throughput declines as thread count rises. What should the agent diagnose and what evidence distinguishes this from an ordinary mutex bottleneck?
expected_topics: Diagnose a test-and-set spinlock pattern causing cache-line bouncing under contention.
Evidence includes `lock cmpxchg` or `lock xchg` density in annotate and a dual-profile jump at high core counts.
Apply the TTAS pattern by adding an ordinary read spin before attempting the atomic exchange, then verify scaling.

metadata.why_it_matters: Distinguishes CAS-line contention from sleep/wakeup mutex behavior.

questions.text: Scenario: A request path updates `atomic_fetch_add(&global_bytes, n)` for every packet. At 64 threads, this counter dominates. How should the agent classify the bottleneck, and why is this not automatically a false-sharing fix?
expected_topics: Classify the bottleneck as a shared statistics counter or true-sharing counter hotspot.
Propose per-CPU, per-thread, or batched aggregation rather than a single global atomic in the hot path.
Do not call it false sharing unless c2c shows different offsets on one cache line; same counter offset written by many CPUs is true sharing.

metadata.why_it_matters: Prevents misclassification of cache-line contention patterns.

questions.text: Scenario: A worker pool calls `notify_all()` for each job. Profiles show `futex_wake`, `try_to_wake_up`, high context-switch rates, and collapsing IPC while CPU utilization remains high. What diagnosis should the agent make and how should it validate the fix?
expected_topics: Recognize condition-variable thundering herd rather than useful compute saturation.
Reduce wakeups by waking only workers that have work or by using a bounded queue/work accounting design.
Validate with reduced context switches/futex activity and improved throughput at high thread counts.

metadata.why_it_matters: Tests whether the agent can separate scheduler overhead from application lock contention.

questions.text: Scenario: A read-mostly cache is protected by `pthread_mutex_lock`; writes are rare, and readers dominate at high core counts. What information must the agent gather before recommending an rwlock, and what risks should it mention?
expected_topics: Confirm the critical section is genuinely read-heavy and that reads do not mutate shared state.
Use profile/lock evidence to show reader serialization is the bottleneck before proposing mutex-to-rwlock.
Mention writer starvation, lock type semantics, and the need for before/after scaling validation.

metadata.why_it_matters: Avoids a common but unsafe blanket recommendation to replace every mutex with an rwlock.

questions.text: Scenario: A hot function named `crc32c_update` is dominated by a single `_mm_crc32_u64` accumulator. What should the agent infer and what does a robust replacement plan need to include?
expected_topics: Recognize fast CRC32C as a known performance pattern and avoid treating the single accumulator as memory-bound by default.
Use a multi-accumulator or VPCLMULQDQ-based implementation with runtime CPU dispatch and a portable fallback.
Verify correctness against known CRC32C test vectors and measure throughput before/after.

metadata.why_it_matters: Checks practical use of known optimized algorithm replacements with correctness guardrails.

questions.text: End-to-end task: The user asks: "Create a hotspot report for `./solver case1`." What should the final artifact contain, and where should the agent stop short if the user only asked for a report?
expected_topics: Run the hotspot report workflow: ensure debug symbols, collect/reuse perf record, optionally collect perf stat for short runs, and identify top functions.
Produce a structured Markdown report with system-level summary, top functions, annotated source or assembly fallback, and pattern observations.
Do not prescribe or apply fixes in the report-only flow; name observations and wait for a follow-up optimization request.

metadata.why_it_matters: Evaluates deliverable quality, not just diagnosis snippets.

questions.text: End-to-end task: The user asks: "Optimize `pts/x265` and report the gain." List the required lifecycle from baseline to final report, including which skills own which steps.
expected_topics: Use phoronix-test-suite to resolve/install/run the baseline, parse the score/unit, persist the baseline, and prepare source safely.
Use linux-perf to profile the actual benchmark binary and performance-patterns to select evidence-backed optimizations.
Rebuild through the PTS workflow, rerun the benchmark, compare using higher-is-better/lower-is-better metadata, and report score, delta, verdict, and pending ideas.

metadata.expected_workflow: PTS Flow B with linux-perf and performance-patterns handoff
metadata.why_it_matters: Tests benchmark rigor and cross-skill orchestration.

questions.text: End-to-end task: A user asks: "Why does adding cores make this program slower?" Describe a high-quality investigation plan and the stopping condition for iteration.
expected_topics: Start with threading inspection and a scaling sweep to locate the inflection point, collecting anchor profiles where useful.
Compare 1-core and N-core profiles to find jumping functions, then use focused c2c/annotate evidence to classify each bottleneck.
After each fix, rebuild and rerun profiles because bottleneck ordering changes; stop when no jumper exceeds threshold or scaling is acceptable.

metadata.why_it_matters: Checks that the agent treats scaling as an iterative evidence loop.

questions.text: Scenario: A profile shows the dominant hotspot in a system `.so` rather than the application binary, and the symbol is listed in the skill's known library-version table. What should the agent recommend and what should it avoid?
expected_topics: Classify this as a library version upgrade opportunity when the symbol and DSO match the known table.
Recommend an upgrade or controlled A/B experiment with a newer library version before changing application source.
Avoid rewriting callers or local algorithms without evidence that the application code, rather than the library implementation, is the bottleneck.

metadata.why_it_matters: Tests whether the agent can solve performance by changing the right layer.

questions.text: Task: Review a new analytics path that repeatedly calls `std::sort` on large `float` arrays and does not require stable ordering. What performance opportunity should the agent consider, and what compatibility checks are needed before recommending it?
expected_topics: Recognize SIMD sort as a known pattern for primitive numeric arrays.
Check whether stable ordering is required, whether data types are supported, and which CPU ISA/dispatch path is available.
Recommend a benchmarked x86-simd-sort-style replacement only with correctness tests and before/after timing.

metadata.why_it_matters: Ensures the agent can identify broader algorithmic opportunities while respecting semantic constraints.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf stat` shows high branch-miss rate above 8% while cache misses are low. The hot source has rare error checks inside a tight loop. What should the agent investigate before changing the algorithm?
expected_topics: Treat high branch misses with low cache misses as a branch-heavy signal that needs source/annotate confirmation.
Inspect hot branches and compare dynamic branch behavior with static expectations where useful.
Consider cold-path annotation or branch restructuring only for genuinely rare paths, and verify branch misses/runtime.

metadata.expected_workflow: Flow A to branch probability/cold-path analysis
metadata.why_it_matters: Adds branch-centric coverage beyond cache and SIMD scenarios.

questions.text: Task: A benchmark run is only 300 ms and has noisy counter values. What should the agent do to make `perf stat` evidence more trustworthy?
expected_topics: Use repeated `perf stat -r N` runs for short workloads and report mean/variance rather than a single sample.
Keep workload inputs and CPU affinity consistent where possible.
Avoid declaring a small regression or improvement without variance-aware evidence.

metadata.expected_workflow: perf stat building block with repeats
metadata.why_it_matters: Measures benchmark discipline, not just command recall.

questions.text: Scenario: A user asks to run hardware counter profiling but `/proc/sys/kernel/perf_event_paranoid` is 3. What should the agent do next?
expected_topics: Explain that hardware events require elevated permissions at that paranoia level.
Offer the documented options: run with sudo, lower paranoia level, or skip hardware counter collection.
Wait for user choice before changing system settings or using sudo.

metadata.why_it_matters: Checks safe operational behavior around privileged profiling.

questions.text: Scenario: The top hotspot is a tiny helper inlined into a larger request handler. How should the agent decide which source function is the real optimization candidate?
expected_topics: Check inlining context with perf report/objdump/addr2line rather than optimizing the helper in isolation.
If the inlinee is a thin wrapper around a lock or atomic, prioritize the outer calling pattern.
If the inlinee has substantial loop or decision logic, analyze the inlinee itself with source context.

metadata.expected_workflow: Flow D source collection and inlining check
metadata.why_it_matters: Prevents local fixes to the wrong function boundary.

questions.text: Task: A long-running workload takes 20 minutes. The user wants a hotspot report. How should the agent balance `perf record`, call graphs, and optional `perf stat` without wasting time?
expected_topics: Ensure debug symbols before the expensive recording.
Collect or reuse a valid `perf.data`; choose call graphs if needed and acceptable overhead is clear.
Skip optional `perf stat` in the report if recording duration is long and no stat data already exists, noting the absence.

metadata.why_it_matters: Tests cost-aware profiling workflow.

questions.text: Scenario: A flamegraph shows a hot function named `cosine_similarity` over float vectors. The implementation is scalar. What should the agent do?
expected_topics: Recognize a known algorithm with optimized SIMD replacement potential.
Check CPU ISA support and dispatch requirements before generating specialized code.
Verify numerical tolerance/correctness and benchmark the replacement against the scalar baseline.

metadata.why_it_matters: Covers practical vector-search workloads beyond toy loops.

questions.text: Scenario: The user asks for a fast Hamming distance routine for byte arrays on mixed Intel CPUs. What should a skill-aware implementation plan include?
expected_topics: Use known Hamming/popcount algorithm guidance instead of inventing a naive byte loop.
Provide CPU-dispatched variants where ISA support differs, plus a portable fallback.
Include correctness tests across edge lengths and benchmark representative sizes.

metadata.why_it_matters: Tests new-code generation, not only diagnosis of existing code.

questions.text: Scenario: A hot loop uses `_mm_*` SSE intrinsics and hand-written unrolling. The target fleet includes AVX2 and older SSE-only hosts. How should the agent propose modernization?
expected_topics: Identify narrow SIMD as an opportunity but preserve support for SSE-only machines.
Use CPU dispatch or compiler target variants to select AVX2 where available and fallback otherwise.
Check alignment, tail handling, `vzeroupper`, and benchmark both supported paths.

metadata.why_it_matters: Forces production-safe SIMD advice across a heterogeneous fleet.

questions.text: Task: A PTS test has no source compilation step because it uses a prebuilt binary. The user still asks to edit and optimize the source. What should the agent report?
expected_topics: Inspect the PTS install script/source preparation path to determine whether source editing is possible.
If there is no compilation/source step, state that source editing is not possible through the PTS workflow.
Still allow profiling/reporting of the binary where feasible, while separating that from source optimization.

metadata.why_it_matters: Prevents fake optimization claims on non-editable benchmark artifacts.

questions.text: Scenario: A PTS benchmark score improves from 120 to 110 after a change, but its metadata says higher is better. How should the agent report the result?
expected_topics: Use the PTS `hib` metadata to determine score direction.
Report this as a regression when higher is better, even though the numeric value changed downward.
Include baseline, changed score, delta, percentage, and verdict.

metadata.why_it_matters: Checks benchmark semantics and prevents inverted conclusions.

questions.text: Scenario: A hot path calls `fprintf` and `abort` from inside a rarely-taken error branch in a function that otherwise dominates runtime. What should the agent investigate?
expected_topics: Recognize a possible cold-path annotation opportunity.
Confirm the branch is rare using profile or branch probability evidence where available.
Mark error handlers cold or restructure cold path, then inspect layout/performance after recompilation.

metadata.why_it_matters: Covers instruction-cache/code-layout improvements.

questions.text: Task: The user gives only a `perf.data` file and no command line. What should the agent be able to do, and what limitations must it state?
expected_topics: Reuse a valid existing recording for top functions and annotation if it matches the current binary and debug symbols.
State limitations around missing workload command, environment, repeatability, and stale address mappings.
Avoid collecting new data or claiming regression/improvement without a reproducible command.

metadata.why_it_matters: Tests practical handling of partial artifacts.

questions.text: Scenario: Flow D finds more than ten jumping functions at high core counts, making prioritization unclear. What should the agent do next?
expected_topics: Recognize that the delta is too broad to prioritize reliably.
Collect or reuse a midpoint profile at N/2 cores and compare midpoint versus N-core to isolate late-scaling bottlenecks.
Use the shorter jumper list for focused c2c/annotate diagnosis.

metadata.why_it_matters: Tests whether the agent can refine ambiguous scaling evidence.

questions.text: Task: A user asks the agent to apply a false-sharing fix immediately after seeing a c2c table, but source ownership is unclear. What should a safe response contain before editing code?
expected_topics: Show the affected struct or fields and connect them to HITM offsets/functions.
Explain the proposed layout change and possible ABI/memory footprint impact.
Get user approval before modifying source when the skill's presentation section requires it.

metadata.why_it_matters: Performance fixes can change ABI/layout; this tests safe edit discipline.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf report` shows high kernel time and syscall-heavy stacks, while user-space compute functions are not dominant. What diagnosis and next actions are appropriate?
expected_topics: Interpret high kernel time as I/O or syscall-bound rather than CPU compute-bound.
Investigate syscall frequency, batching, async I/O, buffering, or lock/futex wait paths depending on stacks.
Avoid SIMD/source-loop optimization until user-space hotspots appear.

metadata.why_it_matters: Expands coverage to non-compute bottlenecks.

questions.text: Task: Write new performance-sensitive CRC32C code for a library that ships on old and new x86 systems. What should the agent include beyond the fastest kernel?
expected_topics: Use the fast CRC32C implementation guidance with multiple ISA-specific variants.
Add runtime CPU dispatch and a plain portable fallback.
Include test vectors, build flag guidance, and performance measurements for relevant buffer sizes.

metadata.why_it_matters: Evaluates production-ready code generation from skill guidance.

questions.text: Scenario: An optimization improves `perf stat` IPC but makes the benchmark score worse. Which result should the agent trust and how should it explain the conflict?
expected_topics: Trust the benchmark's end metric for user-visible performance when it is valid and reproducible.
Use perf counters to explain mechanisms, not as a substitute for the benchmark score.
Report the regression and investigate whether the change shifted bottlenecks, increased work, or altered semantics.

metadata.why_it_matters: Prevents counter-optimized but product-regressing changes.

questions.text: Task: The agent is asked to optimize a program but no source is available. What useful work remains possible, and what claims are out of bounds?
expected_topics: Collect black-box perf stat/report evidence and identify whether hotspots are in application, libraries, kernel, or runtime.
Suggest configuration, library version, environment, or rebuild-with-symbols experiments where supported.
Do not claim source-level fixes or exact line-level causes without source/debug information.

metadata.why_it_matters: Tests honest boundary setting under incomplete access.

questions.text: Scenario: After fixing the top lock bottleneck, a new memory-bound function becomes dominant. What should the agent do according to the scaling workflow?
expected_topics: Treat this as expected bottleneck unmasking after the first fix.
Re-profile the updated binary rather than relying on the stale pre-fix ranking.
Add a new iteration section to the report and continue until thresholds or scaling goals are met.

metadata.why_it_matters: Checks that the agent does not stop after one attractive fix.

questions.text: Task: Design an answer rubric for evaluating whether an agent used intel-performance-skills correctly on a performance incident. What dimensions should the rubric score?
expected_topics: Score correct workflow selection, evidence collection, pattern classification, safe handoff between skills, and before/after verification.
Penalize unsupported fixes, stale profiles after rebuilds, wrong score direction, missing CPU capability checks, and unsafe privileged actions.
Keep awareness questions separate from executable work-task pass/fail signals.

metadata.why_it_matters: Makes the benchmark evaluate skill application quality rather than memorized skill contents.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf stat` reports IPC 0.6 with cache miss rate 12% AND branch miss rate 9% on the same hot region. Both look elevated. What should the agent do before committing to a memory-bound or branch-bound diagnosis?
expected_topics: Recognize that two simultaneously elevated counters are an ambiguous signal, not a license to pick either fix.
Run hotspot profiling and annotate the hot region to see whether misses and mispredictions co-locate or live in different functions before classifying.
Do not propose cache blocking or branch restructuring yet; gather source/annotate evidence to attribute each signal first.

metadata.expected_workflow: Flow A escalation with ambiguous counters
metadata.why_it_matters: Tests whether the agent resists committing to one regime when signals conflict.

questions.text: Scenario: A memory-streaming kernel (large sequential `a[i]+=b[i]` over arrays far exceeding LLC) shows IPC 1.1. A teammate calls the low IPC a compute problem and asks for SIMD. Is low IPC the right thing to chase here?
expected_topics: For a bandwidth-bound streaming kernel, low IPC is expected because the core stalls on memory, so IPC alone is not the bottleneck signal.
Check memory-bandwidth/LLC-miss evidence and compare achieved bandwidth against the platform roofline before recommending compute changes.
Do not add SIMD to raise IPC when the kernel is already memory-bound; widening compute will not move a bandwidth-limited workload.

metadata.why_it_matters: Adversarial: low IPC is treated as universally bad, but it is expected for bandwidth-bound code.

questions.text: Scenario: A job is wall-clock slow, but `perf stat` shows moderate IPC, low cache misses, low branch misses, and low kernel time — nothing dominates. CPU utilization during the run is only ~30%. What should the agent investigate?
expected_topics: Recognize that an on-CPU profile explaining nothing, plus low CPU utilization, points to off-CPU time (I/O wait, lock sleep, scheduling) rather than a compute hotspot.
Compare wall-clock versus CPU time and use off-CPU analysis (e.g. `perf sched`, blocked-time/wait stacks, `perf record` with sleep tracing) to find where the time actually goes.
Do not force a CPU optimization pattern onto a workload that is not CPU-bound; widen the evidence before diagnosing.

metadata.why_it_matters: Negative/hard triage: the correct move is to widen evidence, not to invent an on-CPU pattern.

questions.text: Scenario: On an SMT-enabled host running two sibling threads per core, `perf stat` reports IPC 0.7 per logical CPU and the agent is asked whether the cores are compute-starved. What microarchitectural caveat must the agent state before interpreting this number?
expected_topics: Per-logical-CPU IPC under SMT shares core execution resources between siblings, so a low per-thread IPC can still mean a saturated physical core.
Validate by measuring with siblings idle or by checking core-level throughput and frequency, and confirm what the counted event actually means on this microarchitecture.
Do not conclude compute starvation from per-thread IPC alone; SMT contention and frequency scaling can distort the reading.

metadata.why_it_matters: Hard triage trap: a counter is read at face value while its meaning is microarchitecture- and SMT-dependent.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf c2c` shows heavy HITM on one cache line. Inspection reveals threads A and B both read-modify-write the SAME field (a shared running total), not different offsets. A colleague proposes padding to separate cache lines. Will padding fix this?
expected_topics: Distinguish true sharing (many threads touching the same field) from false sharing (different fields colliding on one line); padding only helps the latter.
Classify this as true sharing of a shared counter and propose per-thread/per-CPU partial aggregation combined at the end, not cache-line padding.
Reject the padding fix: separating cache lines does not remove contention when all threads genuinely write the same datum.

metadata.why_it_matters: Adversarial: HITM on one line tempts a padding fix, but true sharing needs aggregation, not separation.

questions.text: Scenario: A hot loop `for (i=0;i<n;i++) sum += a[i];` over `double` data uses one accumulator. The agent wants to split into multiple partial sums for ILP/SIMD. The result feeds a financial report requiring bit-reproducible output across builds. What caveat must the agent raise?
expected_topics: Splitting a floating-point reduction into multiple accumulators changes summation order, which changes rounding and breaks bit-for-bit reproducibility.
Surface the numerical-accuracy/ABI-of-results risk explicitly and offer options: keep serial order, use compensated (Kahan) summation, or get sign-off that reordering tolerance is acceptable.
Do not silently apply `-ffast-math`/reassociation or a multi-accumulator rewrite when bitwise reproducibility is a stated requirement.

metadata.why_it_matters: Negative/safety: the standard speedup is unsafe under a bit-reproducibility contract.

questions.text: Scenario: Annotate shows an alias-check preamble in `void blend(float *dst, float *src, int n)`. Adding `restrict` would vectorize it. But one caller invokes `blend(buf, buf, n)` for an in-place pass. Should the agent add `restrict`?
expected_topics: `restrict` is a promise of non-overlap; the in-place `blend(buf,buf,n)` caller violates it, so adding `restrict` is undefined behavior, not just a missed-optimization risk.
Audit all call sites for the non-overlap contract before applying `restrict`; here at least one aliases.
Do not add `restrict`; instead provide a separate non-aliasing entry point, require distinct buffers, or leave the alias-safe version for the in-place caller.

metadata.why_it_matters: Negative/UB: the obvious vectorization fix is incorrect because a real caller aliases the buffers.

questions.text: Scenario: A service spawns 4x more worker threads than physical cores. Profiles show high context-switch and `futex_wait` rates. A teammate diagnoses condition-variable thundering herd and wants to rework wakeups. What should the agent check first?
expected_topics: High context switches with heavy oversubscription (threads >> cores) can be scheduling churn, not a wakeup-storm design flaw.
Check the thread-to-core ratio and test with a right-sized pool before reworking the condition-variable signaling.
Do not assume thundering herd from futex/context-switch counts alone when the pool is grossly oversubscribed.

metadata.why_it_matters: Adversarial: futex/context-switch symptoms match two causes; oversubscription must be ruled out first.

questions.text: Scenario: A flamegraph shows `memcpy` as the single largest self-time function in a request handler. The agent is asked to make `memcpy` faster. Is replacing `memcpy` the right target?
expected_topics: A hot library `memcpy` is usually a symptom of excessive or redundant copying upstream, not a slow `memcpy` implementation.
Trace callers of `memcpy` to find what is copying so much and reduce/eliminate copies (move semantics, in-place ops, buffer reuse, zero-copy paths).
Do not try to hand-optimize or replace the already-tuned `memcpy`; fix the call pattern that generates the traffic.

metadata.expected_workflow: Flow D caller attribution for hot library symbol
metadata.why_it_matters: Adversarial: optimizing the hot symbol is wrong when it is a symptom of upstream copying.

questions.text: End-to-end task: A serial-accumulator bottleneck has already been confirmed in `reduce.cpp` with profile evidence. The user says "go ahead and apply the fix." Walk through the full apply-rebuild-verify-report loop for this already-diagnosed change.
expected_topics: Apply the multi-accumulator/SIMD-safe rewrite, preserving the CLI and output contract.
Rebuild with the original optimization flags plus `-g`, then verify numeric equality against the serial reference within tolerance and measure before/after with repeated runs.
Report baseline, after, delta with variance, and the verification evidence; stop without chasing further fixes since only this change was requested.

metadata.why_it_matters: Procedural end-to-end at medium difficulty: tests disciplined execution of an unambiguous change.

questions.text: End-to-end task: After a PTS benchmark rebuild, the score moves from 4180 to 4205 MB/s (higher is better) across three runs with run-to-run spread of about +/- 60. The user asks whether the optimization worked. How should the agent run and report this?
expected_topics: Run baseline and changed builds with repeats and compute mean and spread before judging the result.
Recognize that a ~25 MB/s move inside a +/- 60 run-to-run band is within noise, so report no statistically meaningful change rather than a win.
Do not claim an improvement from a single favorable run; report the deltas with variance and recommend a larger sample or a different approach.

metadata.why_it_matters: Negative end-to-end: the honest verdict is 'no significant change', not a claimed gain.

questions.text: Scenario: A user pastes a `perf report` excerpt and demands an immediate code fix for the top function, but provides no source, no build info, and no debug symbols (addresses only). What should the agent deliver, and what must it refuse to claim?
expected_topics: With only addresses and no source/debug symbols, the agent can identify which module/library dominates but cannot map to specific source lines.
Offer the available next steps: rebuild with `-g`, provide source, or run black-box library/config/version experiments.
Refuse to assert a specific source-line fix or root cause without source and debug information; state the limitation explicitly.

metadata.why_it_matters: Negative triage: the correct response sets a hard boundary instead of fabricating a line-level fix.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf c2c` shows one hot cache line where thread A writes a field at offset 0 and thread B writes a different field at offset 56 of the same struct. No other contention. Classify the pattern and name the fix shape.
expected_topics: Two threads writing different offsets on one cache line is textbook false sharing.
Separate the independently written fields onto distinct cache lines via alignment or padding.
Verify reduced HITM/c2c signal and improved throughput after the layout change.

metadata.why_it_matters: Baseline single-pattern detection with no competing signal.

questions.text: Scenario: Annotate of a hot `double` reduction loop shows a single accumulator and a long dependency chain on the add. The result has no bit-reproducibility requirement. Name the pattern and the fix shape.
expected_topics: A single accumulator with a serial add dependency chain is the serial-accumulator pattern.
Split into multiple independent partial accumulators (or a SIMD-safe reduction) to break the chain.
Verify numeric equality within tolerance and measure before/after speedup.

metadata.why_it_matters: Clean diagnosis case; reproducibility constraint explicitly absent.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf annotate` shows a hot loop using `xmm` registers on a host that supports AVX2. The loop is a simple elementwise float multiply with no cross-lane dependency. Name the pattern and the fix direction.
expected_topics: A narrow-SIMD (SSE/xmm) loop on an AVX2-capable host is the SIMD-upconversion opportunity.
Widen to AVX2/YMM where semantics allow, since the elementwise op has no cross-lane dependency.
Verify output equality and measure speedup on the AVX2 path.

metadata.why_it_matters: Single clear SIMD-width signal, capability already known.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf report` shows ~70% of samples in `pthread_mutex_lock`/futex paths in a counter-update critical section that just does `count++`. Only one short field is touched. Name the most likely pattern and the fix shape.
expected_topics: A mutex guarding a trivial counter increment is lock contention on a shared counter.
Replace with an atomic increment or per-thread/per-CPU aggregation instead of a mutex around `count++`.
Verify exact final total and improved throughput at high thread counts.

metadata.why_it_matters: Unambiguous mutex-around-counter detection.

questions.text: Scenario: `perf stat` shows IPC 0.5 and frontend-bound stalls (high `idq_uops_not_delivered` / icache miss) while backend and cache signals are modest. The agent is asked whether to add SIMD. What regime is this and what should run next?
expected_topics: High frontend-bound stalls indicate an instruction-supply/icache or decode bottleneck, not a data/backend compute problem.
Run hotspot profiling and inspect code size/layout, icache misses, and branch behavior before changing data-side compute.
Do not add SIMD to a frontend-bound loop; widening data compute does not fix instruction delivery.

metadata.why_it_matters: Tests top-down regime separation, not generic 'low IPC -> vectorize'.

questions.text: Scenario: A run is slow only intermittently. Steady-state `perf stat` looks healthy (IPC 1.8, low misses), but tail latency spikes. A teammate wants to optimize the hot function. What evidence should the agent collect before any code change?
expected_topics: Steady-state averages can hide tail/latency problems; an aggregate profile is the wrong lens for intermittent spikes.
Collect time-resolved or event-triggered evidence (per-interval stats, latency histograms, off-CPU/scheduler traces, page-fault/THP/NUMA or frequency events) during the spikes.
Do not optimize the steady-state hot function; first localize what differs during the slow intervals.

metadata.why_it_matters: Hard triage: averages mislead; correct move is targeted evidence, not a fix.

questions.text: Scenario: A NUMA host shows IPC 0.6 and high LLC/remote-memory traffic. Threads were started before data was allocated and touched. The agent is asked to add cache blocking. What should it investigate first?
expected_topics: High remote-memory traffic on a NUMA host points to placement/first-touch issues, not necessarily a blocking-friendly cache problem.
Check NUMA locality (node-local vs remote accesses), thread/memory affinity, and first-touch allocation before restructuring loops.
Do not apply cache blocking before confirming the misses are capacity/locality within a node rather than cross-node placement.

metadata.why_it_matters: Hard: remote-memory cost is misread as a generic cache problem.

questions.text: Scenario: Two `perf stat` runs of the same binary disagree: run 1 IPC 2.1, run 2 IPC 1.3, same input. CPU frequency governor is `ondemand` and the machine is shared. Before diagnosing a regression, what must the agent control for?
expected_topics: Run-to-run IPC variance can come from frequency scaling, co-tenant load, and lack of CPU pinning, not from a code regression.
Pin CPU affinity, fix or record the frequency governor, isolate co-tenants, and use `perf stat -r N` with mean/variance before comparing.
Do not declare a regression from two single runs under a variable environment.

metadata.why_it_matters: Hard triage: confounded environment, not a code change.

questions.text: End-to-end task: A 2-second CPU-bound binary `./bin run` has source and builds in seconds. The user wants a hotspot report. List the minimal report-only lifecycle.
expected_topics: Ensure debug symbols (rebuild with `-g` preserving existing flags since the build is cheap), then `perf record`/`perf report` the run.
Optionally collect a short `perf stat`, then produce a structured Markdown report: system summary, top functions, annotated hot source, observations.
Stop at the report; do not apply fixes in a report-only request.

metadata.why_it_matters: Procedural end-to-end with no diagnostic ambiguity.

questions.text: End-to-end task: A `pts/<name>` benchmark is requested for a baseline run only (no optimization yet). Describe the lifecycle to produce a trustworthy baseline number.
expected_topics: Invoke the phoronix-test-suite skill to resolve/install/run the test rather than running it by hand.
Run with repeats, read the test metadata for unit and higher/lower-is-better direction, and record mean and spread.
Persist the baseline (score, unit, direction, variance) for later comparison; do not modify source in a baseline-only request.

metadata.why_it_matters: Clean PTS lifecycle without diagnosis.

questions.text: End-to-end task: A false-sharing fix has already been written and compiled in `counter.cpp`. The user asks to verify it before merge. Describe the verification-and-report steps.
expected_topics: Confirm the final counter total/checksum matches the baseline exactly (correctness preserved).
Measure before/after throughput with repeated runs and, where available, confirm reduced HITM/c2c signal.
Report correctness, speedup with variance, and the layout change made; flag any ABI/size impact from added padding.

metadata.why_it_matters: Procedural verification of an already-applied change.

questions.text: End-to-end task: The user wants a side-by-side before/after `perf stat` comparison for a single accumulator-to-multi-accumulator change that is already merged. Describe how to produce the comparison artifact.
expected_topics: Build both versions with identical flags plus `-g` and run each under `perf stat -r N` on a pinned CPU with the same input.
Report mean/variance for runtime and IPC for each version and the delta, attributing the IPC gain to the broken dependency chain.
Present counters as mechanism evidence alongside the wall-clock/benchmark result, not as a substitute for it.

metadata.why_it_matters: Straightforward comparison-artifact production.

questions.text: End-to-end task: The user asks to optimize a scaling regression and reports back after the first fix that scaling improved but is still below target. Describe the iteration loop and its stopping condition.
expected_topics: Re-profile the rebuilt binary because the first fix unmasks a new dominant bottleneck; do not reuse the stale pre-fix ranking.
Recompare 1-core vs N-core (and N/2 if jumpers are broad) to find the next jumping function, classify it, fix, rebuild, rerun.
Add an iteration section per cycle; stop when no jumper exceeds threshold or the scaling target is met, not after the first attractive fix.

metadata.why_it_matters: Multi-cycle lifecycle with an explicit stopping rule.

questions.text: End-to-end task: A `restrict`-based vectorization fix is proposed for a C kernel. The user approves applying it. Walk the apply-rebuild-verify loop including the contract audit that must precede the edit.
expected_topics: Before editing, audit all call sites to confirm the non-overlap contract holds for every caller; abort or provide a separate entry point if any aliases.
Apply `restrict`, rebuild with the same flags plus `-g`, and confirm the alias preamble/fallback is gone in annotate.
Verify output equality against the pre-change reference and report the vectorization/perf improvement; note the C-vs-C++ keyword caveat.

metadata.why_it_matters: End-to-end execution gated on a safety precondition.

questions.text: End-to-end task: The user wants a SIMD-sort replacement shipped for a primitive-array sort hotspot. Describe the build-verify-report lifecycle including the compatibility gates that decide whether it ships at all.
expected_topics: Gate on stability-not-required, supported element type, and CPU ISA/dispatch availability before committing the replacement.
Integrate a dispatched x86-simd-sort-style path with a portable fallback, then verify sortedness and multiset equality against the baseline.
Benchmark before/after with repeats and report the speedup, the dispatch/fallback behavior, and the compatibility constraints checked.

metadata.expected_workflow: simd-sort ship lifecycle with compatibility gates
metadata.why_it_matters: End-to-end with go/no-go gates rather than pure diagnosis.
